schools.summary: The LORD speaking concerning the penalties for the abominations: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses, saying' — the LORD speaking to Moses, now to give the penalties for the abominations forbidden in the previous chapter (the punishments for Molech worship, the familiar spirits, the incest and adultery and the rest, ch. 20, the speaking, God's command, the new section, the occasion, after the laws of holiness and the prohibitions of ch. 18-19, now the penalties annexed, that the laws might be enforced and the abominations purged from among the people, God himself fixing the penalties, showing the gravity of these sins and his holiness that will not leave them unpunished, the sanctions that guard his holy law); the LORD speaking to Moses (the penalties annexed to the laws) — the new section (the punishments for the abominations); the LORD spake unto Moses — the penalties introduced (the providence, God fixing the punishments, the holiness that guards the law) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott.

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying
the LORD speaking concerning the penalties for the abominations
God fixing the punishments for the abominations forbidden in ch. 18-19 (ch. 20)

schools.summary: The Molech death penalty: 'again, thou shalt say to the children of Israel, Whosoever he be of the children of Israel, or of the strangers that sojourn in Israel, that giveth any of his seed unto Molech; he shall surely be put to death: the people of the land shall stone him with stones' — the death penalty — 'he shall surely be put to death: the people of the land shall stone him with stones': whoever, Israelite or stranger, gave his seed to Molech to be put to death by stoning at the hands of the people (the witnesses noting the significance — the capital penalty for the Molech worship forbidden in Leviticus 18:21, the giving of children to the idol, the abomination of child sacrifice, now visited with death, the lesson — the gravity of the sin, the most dreadful of the abominations, judged with the people's own hands, stoned, the whole community made to purge the evil, the people of the land shall stone him, the public execution, the people themselves the executioners, that all might share the abhorrence and the cleansing, the binding of native and stranger alike, the law over all in the land, the figure — the holiness of God that cannot endure the sacrifice of children to idols, the severe judgment on the profaning of his name and the destruction of the seed he claims, the warning of the wrath against idolatry and the shedding of innocent blood); the Molech worshipper, native or stranger (the giving of the seed to the idol, Leviticus 18:21) — the offenders (all in the land bound); the death by stoning at the people's hands (the public execution, the community purging the evil) — the providence (the abomination judged with death, the people the executioners) — Calvin, Wesley, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the Molech death penalty teach?

sides.position: The capital punishment for the abomination forbidden in Leviticus 18:21, the people themselves stoning the offender to purge the evil from among them - the holiness of God that cannot endure the sacrifice of children to idols, the severe judgment on idolatry and the shedding of innocent blood

schools.summary: God setting his face against the Molech worshipper: 'and I will set my face against that man, and will cut him off from among his people; because he hath given of his seed unto Molech, to defile my sanctuary, and to profane my holy name' — God himself setting his face against the Molech worshipper and cutting him off, for he had defiled the sanctuary and profaned God's holy name (the set my face against, God's personal opposition, his wrath, the cut him off, the divine excision, even if the people failed to execute, the defile my sanctuary, the pollution of the holy place by the idolatry, the profane my holy name, the dishonoring of God by giving his seed to the idol, the figure, the divine wrath against the idolatry, God's own hand against the offender even beyond the human penalty, the gravity of defiling the sanctuary and profaning the name, the jealousy of God for his holiness, Ezekiel 20:39); God's face set against the offender and the cutting off (the divine judgment, even where the human failed) — the wrath (God's personal opposition); 'to defile my sanctuary, and to profane my holy name' (the defiling of the sanctuary and the profaning of the name, the ground of the wrath, the figure, the dishonoring of God, the jealousy for his holiness, Ezekiel 20:39, the providence, the offender cut off by God) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

schools.summary: The warning against the people who hide their eyes from the offender: 'and if the people of the land do any ways hide their eyes from the man, when he giveth of his seed unto Molech, and kill him not' — if the people connived at the Molech worship, hiding their eyes and failing to execute the offender (the hide their eyes, the deliberate overlooking, the connivance at the sin, the kill him not, the failure to execute the penalty, the figure, the guilt of the community that tolerates the sin, the connivance at evil making the people partakers, the danger of winking at sin, the responsibility of the community to purge the evil, not to tolerate it, the corporate guilt of those who let the abomination go unpunished); the people hiding their eyes (conniving at the Molech worship) — the connivance (the toleration of the sin); 'when he giveth of his seed unto Molech, and kill him not' (the failure to execute, the penalty not carried out, the figure, the community partaking in the guilt by toleration, the providence, the connivance judged, God himself acting, vv.5) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: God acting against the offender and his family: 'then I will set my face against that man, and against his family, and will cut him off, and all that go a whoring after him, to commit whoredom with Molech, from among their people' — if the people failed, God himself setting his face against the offender and his family, cutting them off, and all who followed him in the Molech idolatry (the set my face against the man and his family, God's judgment reaching the household that shared the guilt, the all that go a whoring after him, the followers in the idolatry, the whoredom with Molech, idolatry as spiritual adultery, the figure, God's own judgment where the human failed, the idolatry as spiritual whoredom, the unfaithfulness to God figured as adultery, Exodus 34:15-16, the household and followers cut off, the corporate judgment on the idolatry); God's judgment on the offender and his family (where the human failed) — the wrath (God's own cutting off); 'all that go a whoring after him, to commit whoredom with Molech' (the idolatry as spiritual adultery, the unfaithfulness to God, Exodus 34:15-16, the figure, the idolatry as whoredom, the followers judged, the providence, God's judgment on the idolaters) — JFB, Gill.

schools.summary: The penalty for turning to familiar spirits and wizards: 'and the soul that turneth after such as have familiar spirits, and after wizards, to go a whoring after them, I will even set my face against that soul, and will cut him off from among his people' — whoever turned to those with familiar spirits (mediums) or wizards (sorcerers), to seek them, God setting his face against and cutting off (the familiar spirits and wizards, the mediums and sorcerers, the seeking of the dead and the powers of darkness, the go a whoring after them, the spiritual unfaithfulness, idolatry, the set my face against, God's wrath, the figure, the spiritual whoredom of seeking the familiar spirits instead of God, the unfaithfulness of turning to darkness, 'should not a people seek unto their God? for the living to the dead?' Isaiah 8:19, the sin of Saul who sought the witch of Endor, 1 Samuel 28:7; 1 Chronicles 10:13); the turning to the mediums and sorcerers (the seeking of darkness, Isaiah 8:19) — the unfaithfulness (the spiritual whoredom); 'I will even set my face against that soul, and will cut him off from among his people' (God's wrath and cutting off, the judgment on the seeking of darkness, the figure, the spiritual whoredom judged, 1 Samuel 28:7, the providence, the seeker of familiar spirits cut off) — Clarke, Gill.

schools.summary: The call to holiness grounded in the covenant God: 'sanctify yourselves therefore, and be ye holy: for I am the LORD your God' — the call — 'Sanctify yourselves therefore, and be ye holy: for I am the LORD your God': in the midst of the penalties, the call to sanctify themselves and be holy, grounded in 'I am the LORD your God' (the witnesses noting the significance — the keynote of the Holiness Code, the call to sanctify themselves, to be holy, set apart, pure, in contrast with the abominations forbidden, the lesson — the holiness required of God's people because of who he is, the sanctification flowing from the covenant relation, 'be ye holy; for I am holy,' Leviticus 11:44; 1 Peter 1:15-16, 'as he which hath called you is holy, so be ye holy in all manner of conversation,' the sanctify yourselves, the active consecration, the putting away of all defilement, the cleansing of themselves, 'let us cleanse ourselves from all filthiness of the flesh and spirit, perfecting holiness in the fear of God,' 2 Corinthians 7:1, the for I am the LORD your God, the ground and motive, the covenant relation demanding holiness, the figure — the call to holiness in the midst of the warnings against sin, the positive consecration that answers to the negative prohibitions, the people set apart to a holy God); the active sanctifying of themselves (the consecration, the putting away of defilement, 2 Corinthians 7:1) — the call (the positive holiness required); the holiness grounded in the covenant God (the call to be holy because he is the LORD, 1 Peter 1:15-16) — the ground (the covenant relation demanding holiness, the providence, the call to holiness, the LORD who sanctifies, vv.8) — Calvin, Keil, Gill.

schools.summary: The LORD who sanctifies his people: 'and ye shall keep my statutes, and do them: I am the LORD which sanctify you' — the keeping of the statutes joined to the declaration 'I am the LORD which sanctify you' (the keep my statutes, and do them, the obedience required, the I am the LORD which sanctify you, God himself the sanctifier, the one who makes his people holy, the figure, the holiness not of themselves only but of God who sanctifies them, the obedience flowing from the God who works the holiness, the truth that sanctification is God's work in his people, 'the very God of peace sanctify you wholly,' 1 Thessalonians 5:23, 'it is God which worketh in you both to will and to do,' Philippians 2:13, the LORD who both commands and works the holiness); the keeping and doing of the statutes (the obedience required) — the obedience (the doing of the law); 'I am the LORD which sanctify you' (God himself the sanctifier, the one who works the holiness, the figure, sanctification God's work, 1 Thessalonians 5:23; Philippians 2:13, the providence, the LORD who sanctifies, the holiness his gift and work) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

schools.summary: The death penalty for cursing parents: 'for every one that curseth his father or his mother shall be surely put to death: he hath cursed his father or his mother; his blood shall be upon him' — the death penalty — 'every one that curseth his father or his mother shall be surely put to death: his blood shall be upon him': whoever cursed his father or mother to be put to death, his blood upon his own head (the witnesses noting the significance — the capital penalty for cursing the parents, the grave sin of dishonoring father and mother, the breach of the fifth commandment, Exodus 20:12, visited with death, the lesson — the gravity of the honor due to parents, the cursing of the parent so heinous that it incurred death, the foundation of the family order and of all authority, 'honour thy father and thy mother,' and the curse on him who set light by them, Deuteronomy 27:16, the New Testament confirmation — the Lord Jesus cites this very law against the Pharisees who made void the commandment by their tradition, 'For God commanded, saying, Honour thy father and mother: and, He that curseth father or mother, let him die the death,' Matthew 15:4; Mark 7:10, the his blood shall be upon him, the guilt on his own head, the just penalty, the figure — the sacredness of the parental honor, the foundation of the family and society, guarded by the severest penalty, the gravity of the sin that despises the parent God set over us); the cursing of the parent (the dishonor, the breach of the fifth commandment, Exodus 20:12) — the gravity (the heinous sin, the New Testament use, Matthew 15:4); 'his blood shall be upon him' (the guilt on his own head, the just penalty, the offender bearing his own blood, the figure, the just judgment, the sacredness of the parental honor, Deuteronomy 27:16, the providence, the offender justly put to death) — Calvin, Wesley, Clarke, Ellicott, Gill.

sides.position: The gravity of dishonoring father and mother, the breach of the fifth commandment (Exodus 20:12) so heinous it incurred death - the law the Lord Jesus cites against the Pharisees (Matthew 15:4; Mark 7:10), the sacredness of the parental honor as the foundation of the family and society

schools.summary: The death penalty for adultery: 'and the man that committeth adultery with another man's wife, even he that committeth adultery with his neighbour's wife, the adulterer and the adulteress shall surely be put to death' — the death penalty — 'the adulterer and the adulteress shall surely be put to death': both the adulterer and the adulteress to be put to death (the witnesses noting the significance — the capital penalty for adultery, the breach of the seventh commandment, Exodus 20:14, visited with death for both the man and the woman, the lesson — the gravity of adultery, the sin against the marriage bond and the neighbour, the violation of the covenant of marriage, so grave that both parties die, the marriage held sacred by the severest sanction, the both put to death, the equal guilt of both, neither spared, the New Testament reference — this the law the scribes and Pharisees invoked against the woman taken in adultery, 'Moses in the law commanded us, that such should be stoned,' whom the Lord Jesus did not condemn but sent away with 'go, and sin no more,' John 8:3-11, showing the grace that came by Christ alongside the law's just demand, the figure — the sanctity of marriage guarded by the severest penalty, the gravity of the sin that breaks the marriage covenant, yet the grace of Christ for the penitent, 'whoremongers and adulterers God will judge,' Hebrews 13:4); the adultery with the neighbour's wife (the breach of the seventh commandment, Exodus 20:14) — the sin (against the marriage bond); 'the adulterer and the adulteress shall surely be put to death' (both put to death, the equal guilt, John 8:3-11, the figure, the sanctity of marriage guarded, yet the grace of Christ for the penitent, the providence, the gravity of adultery, the marriage held sacred) — Calvin, Keil, Henry, Gill.

debates.question: What does the death penalty for adultery teach?

schools.summary: The death penalty for lying with the father's wife: 'and the man that lieth with his father's wife hath uncovered his father's nakedness: both of them shall surely be put to death; their blood shall be upon them' — the man who lay with his father's wife (the stepmother) had uncovered his father's nakedness, and both to be put to death (the father's wife, the stepmother, the sin forbidden in Leviticus 18:8, the uncovered his father's nakedness, the dishonor of the father, the both put to death, the equal penalty, their blood upon them, the guilt on their own heads, the figure, the gravity of the incest with the father's wife, the dishonor of the father, the very sin Paul condemns in the Corinthian, 'delivered unto Satan,' 1 Corinthians 5:1-5, the severe penalty guarding the father's bed); the lying with the father's wife (the dishonor of the father, Leviticus 18:8) — the sin (the incest forbidden); 'both of them shall surely be put to death; their blood shall be upon them' (both put to death, the guilt on their heads, the just penalty, 1 Corinthians 5:1-5, the figure, the gravity of the sin, the father's bed guarded, the providence, the offenders justly put to death) — JFB, Calvin, Gill.

schools.summary: The death penalty for the abomination of sodomy: 'if a man also lie with mankind, as he lieth with a woman, both of them have committed an abomination: they shall surely be put to death; their blood shall be upon them' — the men who committed the homosexual act, called abomination, both to be put to death (the lie with mankind as with a woman, the act forbidden in Leviticus 18:22, the committed an abomination, the strong word, toebah, the both put to death, the equal penalty, their blood upon them, the guilt on their heads, the figure, the gravity of the abomination, the perversion of the created order judged with death, the same strong word 'abomination' as in the prohibition, the holiness of God against the corruption of nature, yet, as Paul adds, 'such were some of you: but ye are washed,' the grace that cleanses the penitent, 1 Corinthians 6:9-11); the act called abomination (the perversion, Leviticus 18:22) — the gravity (the strong word); 'they shall surely be put to death; their blood shall be upon them' (both put to death, the guilt on their heads, the just penalty, the figure, the holiness against the abomination, yet the grace that cleanses the penitent, 1 Corinthians 6:9-11, the providence, the offenders justly put to death) — Calvin, Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

schools.summary: The penalty for lying with the uncle's wife: 'and if a man shall lie with his uncle's wife, he hath uncovered his uncle's nakedness: they shall bear their sin; they shall die childless' — the man who lay with his uncle's wife (the aunt by marriage) uncovered his uncle's nakedness, and they would bear their sin and die childless (the uncle's wife, the aunt by marriage, the sin forbidden in Leviticus 18:14, the they shall bear their sin, the guilt borne, the they shall die childless, the particular judgment, dying without children, or their children not reckoned, the line cut off, the figure, the gravity of the affinity violated, the uncle dishonored, the judgment of childlessness, the line cut off as the punishment, the dishonor of the uncle's marriage, the divine judgment); the lying with the uncle's wife (the uncle dishonored, Leviticus 18:14) — the sin (the affinity violated); 'they shall bear their sin; they shall die childless' (the guilt borne and the judgment of childlessness, the line cut off, the figure, the dishonor judged, the line ending, the providence, the offenders dying childless) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

schools.summary: The penalty for taking the brother's wife: 'and if a man shall take his brother's wife, it is an unclean thing: he hath uncovered his brother's nakedness; they shall be childless' — the man who took his brother's wife (apart from the levirate duty) committed an unclean thing and would be childless (the brother's wife, the sin forbidden in Leviticus 18:16, save the levirate exception, Deuteronomy 25:5, the it is an unclean thing, niddah, the impurity, the uncovered his brother's nakedness, the brother dishonored, the they shall be childless, the judgment, the line cut off, the figure, the gravity of the affinity violated, the brother dishonored, the judgment of childlessness, the same sin as Herod's, who took his brother Philip's wife, Mark 6:18, the brother's bed guarded, the divine judgment of childlessness); the taking of the brother's wife (the unclean thing, Leviticus 18:16) — the sin (the affinity violated); 'he hath uncovered his brother's nakedness; they shall be childless' (the brother dishonored and the judgment of childlessness, the figure, the brother's bed guarded, Mark 6:18, the line cut off, the providence, the offenders childless) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

schools.summary: The charge to keep the statutes lest the land vomit them out: 'ye shall therefore keep all my statutes, and all my judgments, and do them: that the land, whither I bring you to dwell therein, spue you not out' — Israel to keep all God's statutes and judgments and do them, that the land would not vomit them out as it had the nations (the keep all my statutes, the obedience required, the the land spue you not out, the warning, the land vomiting out the defilers, Leviticus 18:25, 28, the figure, the holiness required to dwell in the land, the warning that disobedience would bring the same judgment on Israel as on the nations, the land vomiting out the corrupt, the moral order recoiling against abomination, the same warning as Leviticus 18:28, fulfilled in the exile); the keeping of all the statutes (the obedience required) — the holiness (the way to dwell in the land); 'that the land, whither I bring you to dwell therein, spue you not out' (the land vomiting out the defilers, the warning, Leviticus 18:25, 28, the figure, the moral order recoiling, the warning fulfilled in the exile, the providence, Israel warned, the land guarded) — Henry, Calvin, Gill.

themes: Ye shall therefore keep all my statutes, and all my judgments, and do them: that the land, whither I bring you to dwell therein, spue you not out

schools.summary: The warning against the nations' abhorred customs: 'and ye shall not walk in the manners of the nation, which I cast out before you: for they committed all these things, and therefore I abhorred them' — Israel not to walk in the customs of the nations God cast out, who committed all these abominations, and whom God abhorred (the not walk in the manners of the nation, the customs of the Canaanites forbidden, the for they committed all these things, the abominations, the therefore I abhorred them, God's abhorrence, the cause of the casting out, the figure, the separation from the customs of the nations, the abominations that made God abhor them, the warning to Israel not to follow the abhorred ways, the holiness of God who abhors the corruption, the people called to be unlike the nations); the customs of the nations forbidden (the Canaanites cast out) — the separation (from the abhorred ways); 'for they committed all these things, and therefore I abhorred them' (the abominations and God's abhorrence, the cause of the casting out, the figure, the holiness that abhors the corruption, the providence, the nations abhorred and cast out, Israel warned) — JFB, Keil, Gill.

schools.summary: The promise of the land and the separation of the people: 'but I have said unto you, Ye shall inherit their land, and I will give it unto you to possess it, a land that floweth with milk and honey: I am the LORD your God, which have separated you from other people' — God promising Israel the land of the nations cast out, a land flowing with milk and honey, and declaring that he had separated them from other peoples (the inherit their land, the promise, the land given in place of the nations, the floweth with milk and honey, the fruitfulness, the goodness of the land, Exodus 3:8, the I am the LORD your God, which have separated you, the election and separation of Israel, the figure, the promise of the inheritance, the goodness of God's gift, and the separation of the people unto God, the election that sets Israel apart from the nations, the ground of their holiness, 'a peculiar people,' Deuteronomy 14:2); the promise of the fruitful land (the inheritance, Exodus 3:8) — the gift (the goodness of God); 'I am the LORD your God, which have separated you from other people' (the election and separation of Israel, the people set apart, Deuteronomy 14:2, the figure, the ground of holiness, the separation unto God, the providence, the people separated, the holiness required, vv.25-26) — Clarke, Gill.

schools.summary: The charge to distinguish clean and unclean creatures: 'ye shall therefore put difference between clean beasts and unclean, and between unclean fowls and clean: and ye shall not make your souls abominable by beast, or by fowl, or by any manner of living thing that creepeth on the ground, which I have separated from you as unclean' — Israel to distinguish clean from unclean creatures (Leviticus 11) and not defile themselves with the unclean (the put difference between clean and unclean, the distinction of the food laws, Leviticus 11, the not make your souls abominable, the avoiding of the defilement, the which I have separated from you as unclean, God's own distinction, the figure, the distinction of clean and unclean as part of the holiness and separation of the people, the food laws a daily reminder of the separation unto God, the people taught to discern and to keep themselves from the unclean, the holiness reaching into the daily food, the separation that answers to God's election, vv.24); the distinguishing of clean and unclean (the food laws, Leviticus 11) — the discernment (the holiness in the daily food); 'ye shall not make your souls abominable... which I have separated from you as unclean' (the avoiding of the unclean, God's own separation, the figure, the holiness reaching into the daily food, the separation unto God, the providence, the people kept separate, the holiness daily) — Calvin, Gill.

schools.summary: The call to be holy and severed to be God's own: 'and ye shall be holy unto me: for I the LORD am holy, and have severed you from other people, that ye should be mine' — the holiness and election — 'ye shall be holy unto me: for I the LORD am holy, and have severed you from other people, that ye should be mine': Israel to be holy because the LORD is holy, severed from the nations to be his own (the witnesses noting the significance — the call to holiness grounded in God's holiness, the ground 'for I the LORD am holy,' the people to be holy because he is holy, the severing from other people, the election, the separation unto God, that they should be his, the lesson — the holiness flowing from the election, God severed them from the nations to be his own peculiar people, and therefore they must be holy, 'ye shall be holy; for I the LORD your God am holy,' Leviticus 19:2; 11:44-45; 1 Peter 1:15-16, 'ye are a chosen generation, a royal priesthood, an holy nation, a peculiar people; that ye should shew forth the praises of him who hath called you,' 1 Peter 2:9, the that ye should be mine, the purpose of the election, the people belonging to God, 'I will be their God, and they shall be my people,' Jeremiah 31:33; 2 Corinthians 6:16, the figure — the holiness and the election joined, the people severed from the world to be God's own, called to holiness as the seal and fruit of their belonging to him, the New Testament fulfilment in the church, severed from the world to be holy unto God); the holiness grounded in God's holiness (the people holy because he is holy, Leviticus 19:2; 1 Peter 1:15-16) — the call (the holiness required); 'and have severed you from other people, that ye should be mine' (the severing from the nations to be God's own, the election, the people belonging to God, 1 Peter 2:9, the figure, the holiness flowing from the election, the providence, the people severed to be his, holy unto him) — Calvin, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the call to be holy and severed teach?

sides.position: The holiness grounded in God's holiness (Leviticus 19:2; 1 Peter 1:15-16), the severing from the nations to be his own peculiar people (1 Peter 2:9), the holiness flowing from the election - the people belonging to God (Jeremiah 31:33; 2 Corinthians 6:16), fulfilled in the church severed from the world to be holy unto God

schools.summary: The death penalty for those with familiar spirits and wizards: 'a man also or woman that hath a familiar spirit, or that is a wizard, shall surely be put to death: they shall stone them with stones: their blood shall be upon them' — those who practiced as mediums (having a familiar spirit) or wizards (sorcerers) to be put to death by stoning (the familiar spirit or wizard, the medium, the sorcerer, the practitioner of the occult, forbidden vv.6, the shall surely be put to death, the penalty, the stone them with stones, the public execution, their blood upon them, the guilt on their heads, the figure, the gravity of the occult arts, the practitioners of darkness judged with death, the holiness of God against the seeking of the spirits and the powers of darkness, the severe penalty closing the chapter as a seal against the idolatry and the occult, 'there shall not be found among you... a consulter with familiar spirits, or a wizard... for all that do these things are an abomination unto the LORD,' Deuteronomy 18:10-12); the mediums and wizards put to death (the occult forbidden, vv.6) — the penalty (the practitioners of darkness judged); 'they shall stone them with stones: their blood shall be upon them' (the death by stoning, the guilt on their heads, the figure, the holiness against the occult, Deuteronomy 18:10-12, the providence, the practitioners justly put to death, the chapter sealed) — Clarke, Gill, Wesley.
